metadata.seed: I keep overthinking everything at work, and I'm scared I'm going to get fired even though nobody said anything.

patient.query: I keep overthinking everything at work, and I'm scared I'm going to get fired even though nobody said anything.

automatic_thoughts: I'm scared I'm going to get fired
I keep overthinking everything

entailment: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the worry about being fired is weighing heavily on you. What specific thoughts or situations at work make you feel that this might happen? What evidence do you have that supports or contradicts that worry?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: Yeah, it really is heavy. I guess it’s just... sometimes I feel like my boss isn’t happy with my work, and I can’t shake the feeling that I’m not doing enough. I keep replaying meetings in my head, thinking maybe I said something stupid or that I’m falling behind. I don’t know, it just feels like one little mistake could be it.

automatic_thoughts: my boss isn’t happy with my work
I’m not doing enough
maybe I said something stupid
I’m falling behind
one little mistake could be it

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of worry about how your boss perceives your work. What specific moments or feedback suggest that they’re unhappy, and can you think of any times when they’ve expressed appreciation or support? Looking at both sides might help you see a fuller picture.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess there have been a few times when they’ve said good things about my work, but those moments feel really overshadowed by the times when I felt criticized. Like, just recently, there was a project I thought went well, but then I got some feedback that made me feel like I didn’t meet expectations. It’s like I hold onto that more than the praise, you know

triggers: times when they’ve said good things about my work
feedback that made me feel like I didn’t meet expectations
automatic_thoughts: I hold onto criticism more than praise

entailment: Tends to be sensitive to criticism (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like criticism feels more powerful than praise for you. What evidence do you have that the criticism outweighs the praise? Are there moments when the praise felt meaningful or changed your view?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Tends to be sensitive to criticism
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: I don’t know... I guess it’s just that the criticism sticks with me longer. It’s like a little voice in my head that keeps reminding me of my mistakes. The praise, while nice in the moment, fades away so quickly. I can think of a couple of times it felt good, but they can’t compete with the weight of feeling like I’m always under scrutiny. It’s

automatic_thoughts: the criticism sticks with me longer
a little voice in my head reminds me of my mistakes
praise fades away quickly
I’m always under scrutiny

entailment: Tends to be sensitive to criticism (finding)
Tends to be critical of others (finding)
neutral: Tends to appear indifferent to praise or criticism (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like criticism leaves a lasting impression for you. What specific moments make you feel that you’re always under scrutiny? Are there any times when you’ve felt safe or unjudged, even briefly?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Tends to be sensitive to criticism
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: Yeah, I guess there was that time during a team meeting when someone pointed out a mistake I made. It felt like everyone was focused on me, and I couldn’t shake that feeling afterward. But there are moments, like when I’m working late and it’s just me, where I don’t feel watched. But even then, I start to worry about how my work will be seen the next

automatic_thoughts: everyone was focused on me
I couldn’t shake that feeling
how my work will be seen

neutral: Fear of being in a small group (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like that moment left a strong impression. What clues do you have that the whole team was actually focusing on you? How might someone else have seen the situation? What might be another way to think about how your work will be perceived?

patient.query: I guess when I made that mistake, I just felt all eyes on me, like everyone was waiting for me to respond or defend myself. I mean, maybe they were focused on their own stuff too, and it wasn’t as big of a deal as I made it out to be. It’s just that it felt so personal in the moment. Thinking about how my work gets perceived, I guess

triggers: everyone was waiting for me to respond or defend myself
automatic_thoughts: they were focused on their own stuff too
it wasn’t as big of a deal as I made it out to be
it felt so personal in the moment
how my work gets perceived

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you felt a lot of pressure, yet you also think it might not have been as big as it seemed. What evidence do you have that people were actually waiting for you to respond, and what evidence suggests they were not? Exploring both sides could help you see the situation from a broader perspective.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: Yeah, I see what you mean. I guess in that moment, it felt like everyone was silent and looking at me, which made it feel heavy, like I had to come up with the perfect answer. But then, I remember others were probably caught up in their own thoughts, too. It’s just difficult to separate what’s really happening from how I feel in those moments. It’s like

triggers: everyone was silent and looking at me
automatic_thoughts: I had to come up with the perfect answer
others were probably caught up in their own thoughts

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you felt a lot of pressure in that moment. What makes you think that everyone was watching you? Could there be other ways to interpret what was happening?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: I guess it just felt like all the attention was on me, but maybe it was just my mind racing. I remember just wanting to shrink down and disappear, you know? It’s like I get so caught up in my worries that I overlook what’s actually going on. It’s hard to see things from another angle when I’m lost in my head.

triggers: all the attention was on me
automatic_thoughts: my mind racing
wanting to shrink down and disappear
caught up in my worries
overlook what’s actually going on
hard to see things from another angle

entailment: Disorientation as to people, time and place (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you were feeling very exposed and overwhelmed by your own thoughts. What do you think might have been happening that made you feel like everyone was looking at you? When you say you wanted to shrink down and disappear, what does that feeling look like for you?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Disorientation as to people, time and place
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query: Yeah, it felt really overwhelming, like my heart was racing and I was just stuck. When I say I wanted to shrink down, I mean I just wanted to be invisible, like I wouldn’t have to face everyone. It’s almost like I’d rather blend into the wall than deal with the pressure. It’s hard to describe, but it’s like this mix of shame and anxiety bubbling up

automatic_thoughts: I want to be invisible
I'd rather blend into the wall

llm_response.response: I hear how intense that feeling of wanting to disappear feels. What does being “invisible” look like for you, and what evidence do you have that you can’t manage the pressure if you stay visible? How might you test a different possibility—like trying a small step toward facing the situation—and what might that experience reveal?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Disorientation as to people, time and place
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query:
Being invisible feels like just... not having to deal with anyone’s eyes on me, like I could just go through the motions without judgment. But it’s weird because when I think about it, I actually have managed a lot of pressure before—like those times when I got positive feedback after pushing through. It’s just that moment of feeling exposed that makes everything else fade. 

I guess testing a

automatic_thoughts: not having to deal with anyone’s eyes on me
I could just go through the motions without judgment
I actually have managed a lot of pressure before
those times when I got positive feedback after pushing through

llm_response.response: What evidence do you have that being seen always makes you feel exposed? Can you recall any moments when being visible felt manageable or even empowering? How might you reframe those moments of exposure to see them as opportunities rather than threats?
